reasoning: The finding identifies a real bad practice (json_encode without JSON_HEX_TAG in a script block, with unsanitized stored POST data), but the specific exploit vector claimed is neutralized by PHP's default json_encode behavior. PHP's json_encode() escapes forward slashes by default (/ becomes \/), so the payload </script><script>alert(1)</script> is serialized as "<\/script><script>alert(1)<\/script>". Browsers do not treat <\/script> (with a backslash) as closing a script block per the HTML5 parsing spec, which requires the literal string </script. The remaining unescaped characters (< and >) appear inside a JS string literal within an already-open <script> block where the HTML parser is inactive and does not reparse. U+2028/U+2029 line terminators are escaped by json_encode. Quote injection (breaking out of the JSON string) is also escaped. Auth analysis is accurate: MWF_Config::CAPABILITY = 'edit_pages' (confirmed in class.config.php), the chart page is a registered admin submenu, and only admin-level users can reach _save(). The nonce check via check_admin_referer() is also present. The data flow is: POST -> _sanitize() (no escaping, stores raw) -> update_option() -> get_option() -> json_encode() in <script> -> but default slash escaping blocks the described attack. Finding is a false positive on exploitability despite correctly identifying a poor practice.
